points: Urban local bodies are decentralised government structures in cities and towns that enable local communities to directly participate in managing their areas and making decisions about issues affecting their lives.

Based on population, urban areas are governed by three types of bodies: Municipal Corporations (above 10 lakhs), Municipal Councils (1-10 lakhs), and Nagar Panchayats (small towns). Cities and towns are further divided into smaller units called wards, each with elected representatives and a ward committee.

Urban local bodies manage infrastructure (water supply, roads, streetlights, drainage), solid waste management, public health, business licensing, tax collection, and social-economic development. They also facilitate civic services through Citizen Relationship Management (CRM) systems.

Both urban local bodies and citizens share responsibility for effective governance: bodies must provide services efficiently, while citizens must segregate waste, report problems, pay taxes, and participate actively in community initiatives.

Like rural Panchayati Raj institutions, urban local bodies operate on participatory democracy, but urban systems are more complex due to larger, diverse populations and sophisticated infrastructure needs. Ward committees facilitate citizen participation through health camps, awareness campaigns, and grievance monitoring.

Historical examples include the Madras Corporation (1688, India's oldest municipal institution) and the Indore Municipal Corporation (cleanest city in India for seven years), demonstrating how effective governance and citizen participation transform urban areas.
